name: Joy Eakins
occupation: Founder and President, Cornerstone Data Inc.
education: Mathematics, Computer Science, and Theological Studies (full-ride scholarships for undergraduate and graduate degrees)
family: Married to Eric for 31 years; one son serving in the U.S. Air Force
whoTheyAre:
Joy Eakins is a Wichita business owner and data executive running for governor in 2026. She grew up in difficult circumstances but earned full-ride scholarships for both her undergraduate and graduate degrees, studying mathematics, computer science, and theological studies. After working at well-known technology companies including Bell Labs, Lucent Technologies, and Avaya, she founded Cornerstone Data in 2008, a data analytics consulting firm that has grown to serve clients across the country. She has been married to her husband Eric for 31 years, and their son currently serves in the United States Air Force.

Eakins served on the Wichita Board of Education for District 2 from 2013 to 2018, winning her first race by just 46 votes. She chose not to run for re-election. During her time on the board, she pushed for parents' rights and fiscal responsibility. She also chaired the Wichita Regional Chamber of Commerce in 2023, giving her connections to the Kansas business community.

Her campaign brand is the "Kansas Comeback," and she presents herself as a data-driven problem solver who can bring a business mindset to the governor's office. She describes herself as a "strong conservative and America First patriot" and says her faith is central to who she is.
theirRecord:
Eakins's public record is rooted in education rather than the state legislature. Her most visible work was on the Wichita Board of Education, where she used her data analytics background to argue that Kansas schools could safely reopen during the COVID-19 pandemic. Her campaign says she used Cornerstone Data to prove how students could return to classrooms without unnecessary risk. She also pushed for parental rights in education decisions and fiscal responsibility in school spending.

On the positive side, her business track record is real. She built Cornerstone Data from the ground up after working at major technology firms, and her selection as Wichita Chamber chair shows peer recognition in the business world. Her education background gives her more governing experience than a pure outsider candidate.

On the other hand, her political experience is thin for a governor's race. She served one term on a local school board, won that seat by a razor-thin margin, and did not seek re-election. She has never held state or federal office. Her campaign is overwhelmingly self-funded, with roughly 88 percent of her war chest coming from a single $1 million personal loan, which raises questions about whether she has built broad grassroots support among Kansas voters.
whatYouShouldKnow: Eakins founded Cornerstone Data in 2008 after working at Bell Labs, Lucent Technologies, and Avaya. The firm is a full-service data analytics consultancy based in Wichita with clients across the country.
She served on the Wichita Board of Education for District 2 from roughly 2013 to 2018. She won her first race by just 46 votes over Scott Poor and chose not to run for re-election.

Her campaign is heavily self-funded. Out of approximately $1.14 million raised, $1 million came from a personal loan she made to her own campaign on December 29, 2025. Only about $140,000 came from outside donors.
She chaired the Wichita Regional Chamber of Commerce in 2023, a sign of her standing in the Wichita business community.
At the January 2026 Republican governor's debate, she positioned herself as an outsider, saying that Kansas' established politicians 'have been stewarding our decline.'
She explicitly aligns with President Trump, saying Kansas was on the cusp of a 'golden age' under his leadership but could miss out without a conservative governor.

Her campaign emphasizes 'medical freedom,' arguing that families should make their own medical decisions without government interference. This language grew out of the COVID-era debate over school closures and vaccine mandates.
She describes herself as a devout Christian and says she is a faithful member of River Community Church in the Wichita area.
whereTheyWorship:
Joy Eakins says she is a faithful member of River Community Church. Her campaign biography on kansascomeback.com identifies her as a devout Christian, and her academic background includes a degree in Theological Studies. The company she founded, Cornerstone Data, carries a name with clear Christian roots.

River Community Church appears to be a non-denominational Christian congregation in the Wichita area. Its website is rivercc.org. No other church affiliation was found in public records or news coverage.

church.name: River Community Church
church.details: Eakins's campaign biography identifies her as a faithful member of this congregation. River Community Church is located in the Wichita area.

quotes.text: Families should have the right to make the medical decisions that benefit themselves and their kids, even if those decisions are different from what government bureaucrats recommend.
The United States was on the cusp of a 'golden age' under President Donald Trump, but Kansas could miss out on that good fortune if it failed to elect a conservative governor.
Joy used her data analytics firm to prove how we could get kids back into Kansas classrooms safely.
Kansas' established politicians have been stewarding our decline.

campaignFinance.narrative: Joy Eakins is one of the better-funded candidates in the 2026 Kansas governor's race, but the vast majority of her money comes from her own pocket. She loaned her campaign $1,000,000 on December 29, 2025. Only about $142,000 came from outside contributions. She had spent just $116,269 as of the year-end filing, leaving $1,026,491 in cash on hand. The low spending suggests she is stockpiling money for advertising later in the primary. Her donor list is short, with most individual contributions under $1,100. This fundraising picture means she can compete financially, but it raises questions about how much grassroots support she has built compared to candidates raising money from thousands of small donors.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: The full 18-page itemized donor schedule was not fully reconstructed. Unitemized contributions totaled $561.82.
campaignFinance.source: Kansas Ethics Commission Campaign Finance Reports
